svg.title: Spam Complaint Feedback Loop — How Providers Report Complaints to Senders
svg.desc: Diagram showing the complete spam complaint signal path from recipient clicking Report Spam through provider reputation calculation to sender notification via feedback loops. Compares three fundamentally different feedback mechanisms: Gmail Postmaster Tools provides aggregate trend dashboards only and requires a Feedback-ID header for campaign-level data. Yahoo Complaint Feedback Loop forwards individual complaint messages in ARF format. Microsoft operates two programs through one portal — JMRP forwards individual complaints while SNDS provides IP-level aggregate dashboards. Each provider evaluates sender reputation independently based on their own recipients, so complaint rates can differ across providers for the same campaign. Senders need all three FBLs configured to catch issues wherever they surface.

cc:Work.dc:title: Spam Complaint Feedback Loop — How Providers Report Complaints to Senders
cc:Work.dc:creator: InboxAlly
cc:Work.dc:subject: spam complaint feedback loop, FBL, Gmail Postmaster Tools, Feedback-ID header, Yahoo CFL, complaint feedback loop, Microsoft JMRP, Microsoft SNDS, ARF format, abuse reporting format, spam rate, sender reputation, Report Spam, complaint suppression, email deliverability
cc:Work.dc:description: Cross-provider comparison of spam complaint feedback mechanisms showing Gmail aggregate dashboards versus Yahoo and Microsoft individual complaint forwarding, with the universal complaint signal path and operational response guidance.

svg.text: HOW PROVIDERS REPORT COMPLAINTS TO SENDERS

g.data-description: Universal flow showing what happens when a recipient clicks Report Spam — the complaint enters the provider reputation system, is weighted against total volume, affects all mail from the sender at that provider, and is then routed to the sender via a feedback loop if configured
g.text: Goes to provider first — not to you
One complaint affects your reputation for ALL mail to that provider — not just the reported campaign

svg.text: HOW EACH PROVIDER REPORTS BACK TO YOU

g.aria-label: Gmail Feedback Loop via Postmaster Tools
g.data-description: Gmail provides aggregate trend dashboards only through Google Postmaster Tools. Requires domain verification and Feedback-ID header for campaign-level data. Does not forward individual complaint messages or identify specific complainants.
g.text: Spam rate % · Domain reputation · IP reputation
Spam rate trends, domain + IP reputation scores
No individual complaints · No complainant identities
Verify domain (DNS TXT) · Add Feedback-ID header
Data appears above undisclosed volume threshold
Works for any sender — no IP ownership required
No way to suppress individual complainants.

g.data-description: Yahoo forwards individual complaint messages back to the sender in ARF (Abuse Reporting Format) via the registered complaint address. Sender can identify specific complainants and suppress them.
g.text: Original message returned in ARF format
Requires infrastructure to receive + parse ARF email
Domain-based: verify domain + provide complaint address
Works for any sender — no IP ownership required
Suppress the individual and fix the campaign.

g.aria-label: Microsoft JMRP and SNDS Feedback Programs
g.data-description: Microsoft operates two programs through one portal. JMRP (Junk Mail Reporting Program) forwards individual complaint messages similar to Yahoo CFL. SNDS (Smart Network Data Services) provides IP-level aggregate dashboards. Both are managed through the SNDS portal at sendersupport.olc.protection.outlook.com.
g.text: Two programs, one portal (JMRP lives inside SNDS)
JMRP: individual complaints in ARF (like Yahoo)
SNDS is IP-only — limited value on shared IPs
One portal: register IPs, enable JMRP + SNDS
Verify IP ownership via WHOIS abuse contact email
ESP-owned IPs: ESP handles enrollment + reporting
JMRP = messages. SNDS = dashboards. Enable both.

g.aria-label: Key insight: providers evaluate reputation independently
g.data-description: Each provider evaluates sender reputation independently based on their own recipients — a complaint problem at one provider will not appear at the others
g.text: Each provider evaluates your reputation independently — a problem at one won't show up at the others
The same campaign can spike complaints at Yahoo but look clean at Gmail.

g.data-description: Three-phase operational response: immediately suppress complainants, identify problem campaigns, keep complaint rate below provider thresholds
g.text: WHAT TO DO WITH THE DATA
Yahoo/JMRP: suppress address via ARF report
Gmail: no individual data — monitor aggregate rate
Yahoo/JMRP: match reported message to campaign
Gmail: use Feedback-ID segments to isolate source
